ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and clearly differentiated approach: using structured ontologies and representative phrases as distant supervision to jointly learn word, phrase, and concept embeddings via an adapted skip-gram with negative sampling. Jointly embedding concepts, phrases, and words in a single training procedure and tying concept updates to representative phrase contexts is a non-trivial extension beyond vanilla skip-gram and prior concept-only methods, and the authors compare against a wide set of baselines. Given the summary, this would meaningfully inform experts working on concept/entity representation learning, though it appears as an evolution of existing techniques rather than a radical paradigm shift. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and clearly differentiated idea: using representative phrases drawn from structured ontologies as distant supervision to jointly learn word, phrase, and concept embeddings—an approach that departs from prior purely corpus-driven or separately-trained concept embedding methods. Methodologically it is a non-trivial adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling to propagate supervision from ontology phrases to concept vectors, and the empirical focus on both biomedical concepts and real-world entities strengthens the claim of broader applicability. Given the existing literature (DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, etc.), this is more than an incremental engineering combination but stops short of a fundamentally new paradigm, so it should be judged as a notable contribution rather than a groundbreaking one.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and clearly differentiated approach: using structured ontologies and representative phrases as distant supervision to jointly learn word, phrase, and concept embeddings via an adapted skip-gram with negative sampling. Jointly embedding concepts, phrases, and words in a single training procedure and tying concept updates to representative phrase contexts is a non-trivial extension beyond vanilla skip-gram and prior concept-only methods, and the authors compare against a wide set of baselines. Given the summary, this would meaningfully inform experts working on concept/entity representation learning, though it appears as an evolution of existing techniques rather than a radical paradigm shift.

Methodologically it is a non-trivial adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling to propagate supervision from ontology phrases to concept vectors, and the empirical focus on both biomedical concepts and real-world entities strengthens the claim of broader applicability. Given the existing literature (DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, etc.), this is more than an incremental engineering combination but stops short of a fundamentally new paradigm, so it should be judged as a notable contribution rather than a groundbreaking one.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early; the writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough, so the methodological exposition (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) appears logical. However, presentation gaps remain: hyperparameter settings are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility and leaves some implementation details underspecified. Notation and figures/tables are not flagged as problematic in the summary, but the missing hyperparameter details are a notable clarity omission for readers attempting to replicate the experiments. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and easy to follow—introduction, method, experiments and conclusion flow logically, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough; figures/tables and examples are described as effective and writing quality is good. Presentation is weakened by missing details that affect reproducibility (hyperparameters not reported and no mention of released code). Overall the clarity is high with some minor presentation gaps that should be addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early; the writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough, so the methodological exposition (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) appears logical. However, presentation gaps remain: hyperparameter settings are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility and leaves some implementation details underspecified. Notation and figures/tables are not flagged as problematic in the summary, but the missing hyperparameter details are a notable clarity omission for readers attempting to replicate the experiments.

Presentation is weakened by missing details that affect reproducibility (hyperparameters not reported and no mention of released code). Overall the clarity is high with some minor presentation gaps that should be addressed.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP/representation-learning topic (joint embeddings of concepts, phrases, and words) that clearly fits top NLP/ML venues and is targeted at the right audience. Methodology and evaluation (multiple biomedical and general-domain corpora, standard baselines, Spearman’s ρ and MAP) meet community expectations, but reproducibility concerns weaken the fit: code is not mentioned and hyperparameters/training details are not reported, and some claims hinge on dataset-specific behavior. With explicit release of training hyperparameters, seeds, and code (and additional ablations on sensitivity to corpus size), the paper would be an excellent fit. | B(4): The paper addresses a central NLP/ML topic—learning joint embeddings for concepts, phrases, and words—and presents a weakly-supervised method evaluated extensively (PubMed, clinical notes, Wikipedia/Gigaword) against many strong baselines using standard metrics (Spearman ρ, MAP). Its focus on ontology-aware distant supervision and thorough related-work coverage appeal to both NLP and representation-learning audiences, though the heavy biomedical emphasis and missing details about code/hyperparameter reporting temper its generality. Overall it is a good fit for a top venue with minor scope and reproducibility concerns.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/representation-learning topic (joint embeddings of concepts, phrases, and words) that clearly fits top NLP/ML venues and is targeted at the right audience. Methodology and evaluation (multiple biomedical and general-domain corpora, standard baselines, Spearman’s ρ and MAP) meet community expectations, but reproducibility concerns weaken the fit: code is not mentioned and hyperparameters/training details are not reported, and some claims hinge on dataset-specific behavior. With explicit release of training hyperparameters, seeds, and code (and additional ablations on sensitivity to corpus size), the paper would be an excellent fit.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a central NLP/ML topic—learning joint embeddings for concepts, phrases, and words—and presents a weakly-supervised method evaluated extensively (PubMed, clinical notes, Wikipedia/Gigaword) against many strong baselines using standard metrics (Spearman ρ, MAP). Its focus on ontology-aware distant supervision and thorough related-work coverage appeal to both NLP and representation-learning audiences, though the heavy biomedical emphasis and missing details about code/hyperparameter reporting temper its generality. Overall it is a good fit for a top venue with minor scope and reproducibility concerns.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against a broad set of relevant baselines (e.g., DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, CHIU variants, WORD/PHRASE variants) on shared datasets and standard metrics (Spearman ρ, MAP), and even acknowledges when prior work (CHOI) outperforms it on smaller biomedical data. However, key reproducibility details that affect fairness—hyperparameter settings and code availability—are not reported, which makes it hard to verify that comparisons were run under the same conditions. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly and analyze when their method succeeds or fails, but the lack of experimental-detail disclosure is a material omission for fully fair evaluation. | B(4): The paper benchmarks against a broad and appropriate set of prior methods (e.g., DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, CHIU variants, WORD/PHRASE baselines) across multiple biomedical and general-domain corpora and uses standard metrics (Spearman ρ, MAP). Comparisons appear fair in that same datasets and intrinsic evaluations are used and the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where CHOI outperforms their method on smaller biomedical data. However, important experimental details that affect fairness—most notably absence of reported hyperparameter settings and no mention of released code—could impede exact reproducibility and raise questions about parity in tuning across baselines. Given the thorough related-work discussion and overall coverage, but the minor omissions around experimental transparency, I rate the comparison quality as good with room for improvement.

However, key reproducibility details that affect fairness—hyperparameter settings and code availability—are not reported, which makes it hard to verify that comparisons were run under the same conditions. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly and analyze when their method succeeds or fails, but the lack of experimental-detail disclosure is a material omission for fully fair evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper benchmarks against a broad and appropriate set of prior methods (e.g., DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, CHIU variants, WORD/PHRASE baselines) across multiple biomedical and general-domain corpora and uses standard metrics (Spearman ρ, MAP). Comparisons appear fair in that same datasets and intrinsic evaluations are used and the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where CHOI outperforms their method on smaller biomedical data. However, important experimental details that affect fairness—most notably absence of reported hyperparameter settings and no mention of released code—could impede exact reproducibility and raise questions about parity in tuning across baselines. Given the thorough related-work discussion and overall coverage, but the minor omissions around experimental transparency, I rate the comparison quality as good with room for improvement.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates on multiple corpora (PUBMED, CLINICAL, WIKINYT) and diverse intrinsic tasks with many baselines and metrics, which supports its core claims about learned embeddings. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters reported, code availability not mentioned), and the summary indicates no ablation or sensitivity analyses to isolate contributions of model components or hyperparameters. Related work is described as thorough and the authors acknowledge some failure modes, but the lack of training details and controlled ablations prevents fully convincing empirical validation. | B(4): The paper presents a broad set of experiments across biomedical and general-domain corpora, multiple intrinsic evaluations (Spearman ρ, MAP), many competitive baselines, and even a new real-world entity dataset, which together provide strong empirical support for the main claims. However, there are notable gaps for reproducibility and analysis: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and no explicit ablation or sensitivity studies are described despite the authors noting hyperparameter sensitivity and dataset-dependent failures (e.g., CHOI outperforming on smaller biomedical data). Overall the experimental work is solid and convincing but missing some crucial details and analyses that would make the results fully reproducible and better understood.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on multiple corpora (PUBMED, CLINICAL, WIKINYT) and diverse intrinsic tasks with many baselines and metrics, which supports its core claims about learned embeddings. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters reported, code availability not mentioned), and the summary indicates no ablation or sensitivity analyses to isolate contributions of model components or hyperparameters. Related work is described as thorough and the authors acknowledge some failure modes, but the lack of training details and controlled ablations prevents fully convincing empirical validation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a broad set of experiments across biomedical and general-domain corpora, multiple intrinsic evaluations (Spearman ρ, MAP), many competitive baselines, and even a new real-world entity dataset, which together provide strong empirical support for the main claims. However, there are notable gaps for reproducibility and analysis: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and no explicit ablation or sensitivity studies are described despite the authors noting hyperparameter sensitivity and dataset-dependent failures (e.g., CHOI outperforming on smaller biomedical data). Overall the experimental work is solid and convincing but missing some crucial details and analyses that would make the results fully reproducible and better understood.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This paper tackles an important practical problem—jointly embedding concepts, phrases, and words using weak supervision from ontologies—and demonstrates competitive results across multiple biomedical and general datasets against a broad set of baselines. From my perspective the approach is useful and likely to be adopted for similar tasks, but it does not appear transformative: performance is mixed (e.g., underperforms CHOI on smaller biomedical sets), hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and some conceptual information is not captured, which limits immediate reproducibility and broader impact. The method does, however, open reasonable follow-on directions for improving ontology-driven weak supervision and extending to more abstract domains. | B(3): This paper addresses an important practical problem—learning joint embeddings for concepts, phrases, and words without annotated corpora—using ontologies as distant supervision, and demonstrates competitive performance on biomedical and real-world entity benchmarks. The approach is a useful, incremental advance that makes it easier to incorporate ontology structure into distributional representations and will likely be adopted or extended by researchers working on biomedical NLP and entity representation. However, it is not clearly transformative: some baselines outperform it on smaller biomedical datasets, hyperparameter sensitivity and missing non-lexical concept information limit immediate broad applicability, and code/hyperparameter details are not reported, which tempers reproducibility and rapid uptake.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—jointly embedding concepts, phrases, and words using weak supervision from ontologies—and demonstrates competitive results across multiple biomedical and general datasets against a broad set of baselines. From my perspective the approach is useful and likely to be adopted for similar tasks, but it does not appear transformative: performance is mixed (e.g., underperforms CHOI on smaller biomedical sets), hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and some conceptual information is not captured, which limits immediate reproducibility and broader impact. The method does, however, open reasonable follow-on directions for improving ontology-driven weak supervision and extending to more abstract domains.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important practical problem—learning joint embeddings for concepts, phrases, and words without annotated corpora—using ontologies as distant supervision, and demonstrates competitive performance on biomedical and real-world entity benchmarks. The approach is a useful, incremental advance that makes it easier to incorporate ontology structure into distributional representations and will likely be adopted or extended by researchers working on biomedical NLP and entity representation. However, it is not clearly transformative: some baselines outperform it on smaller biomedical datasets, hyperparameter sensitivity and missing non-lexical concept information limit immediate broad applicability, and code/hyperparameter details are not reported, which tempers reproducibility and rapid uptake.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents experiments that broadly support the main claims (competitive performance on multiple biomedical and entity benchmarks and ontology-consistent geometry), but crucial reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameter settings reported, code availability not mentioned, and no evidence of multiple random seeds or statistical testing). The methodological adaptation of skip-gram appears plausible and comparisons include many baselines, yet the admitted failure on smaller biomedical datasets (CHOI outperforming) and the lack of ablations that isolate contribution of representative-phrase supervision leave some claims only partially validated. Overall the empirical story is promising but incomplete: additional details (hyperparameters, seeds, code), rigorous statistical evaluation, and targeted ablations are needed to fully substantiate the claims. | B(4): The paper's principal claims are supported by empirical evidence across multiple datasets and reasonable baselines, showing competitive performance and ontology-consistent embedding structure. Mathematical formulation appears to be a straightforward adaptation of skip-gram with sensible training updates; no incorrect derivations are indicated in the summary. However, technical completeness is weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance testing, and admitted limitations (competitor CHOI outperforming on small biomedical sets and some non-lexical concept information not captured), which leave reproducibility and some claims under-supported. Overall the method is technically sound but has minor methodological gaps that should be addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents experiments that broadly support the main claims (competitive performance on multiple biomedical and entity benchmarks and ontology-consistent geometry), but crucial reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameter settings reported, code availability not mentioned, and no evidence of multiple random seeds or statistical testing). The methodological adaptation of skip-gram appears plausible and comparisons include many baselines, yet the admitted failure on smaller biomedical datasets (CHOI outperforming) and the lack of ablations that isolate contribution of representative-phrase supervision leave some claims only partially validated. Overall the empirical story is promising but incomplete: additional details (hyperparameters, seeds, code), rigorous statistical evaluation, and targeted ablations are needed to fully substantiate the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's principal claims are supported by empirical evidence across multiple datasets and reasonable baselines, showing competitive performance and ontology-consistent embedding structure. Mathematical formulation appears to be a straightforward adaptation of skip-gram with sensible training updates; no incorrect derivations are indicated in the summary. However, technical completeness is weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance testing, and admitted limitations (competitor CHOI outperforming on small biomedical sets and some non-lexical concept information not captured), which leave reproducibility and some claims under-supported. Overall the method is technically sound but has minor methodological gaps that should be addressed.

main_claims: Proposes a novel weakly-supervised method to jointly embed concepts, phrases, and words using structured ontologies and an unannotated corpus.
The method requires no human corpus annotations and uses representative phrases as distant supervision to train concept embeddings.
Learned embeddings (for biomedical concepts and real-world entities) are competitive with prior concept embedding methods on similarity and relatedness tasks.
The learned vector space reflects ontology structure: concepts and their representative phrases are embedded close together and concepts of the same semantic type tend to cluster.
_summary.methodology: An adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling that jointly learns word (EW), phrase (EP), and concept (EC) embedding matrices by iterating over tagged and untagged corpora; phrases update phrase embeddings from their word contexts and concepts are updated from contexts of their representative phrases (normalized by number of concepts per phrase).
datasets: CLINICAL (clinical notes from Ohio State Wexner Medical Center)
WIKINYT (2015 English Wikipedia + New York Times portion of Gigaword)
Novel dataset of similarity and relatedness of real-world entities (identified by Wikipedia page and text phrase)
metrics: Mean Average Precision (MAP) for neighborhood retrieval
limitations: On smaller biomedical datasets the CHOI method outperforms the proposed method.
Some non-lexical information about concepts is not being captured by the method.
Hyperparameter settings optimized for intrinsic affine evaluations may change and could degrade performance on downstream tasks; hyperparameter selection is important.
Method would benefit from application to more abstract general-domain data to validate generality.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - concept and entity embeddings / representation learning

reviewer_a.name: Reproducibility-Focused Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior machine learning engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating representation learning systems for NLP in both industry and academia; experience implementing large-scale embedding training pipelines and running rigorous empirical comparisons.
traits: Insists on clear, replicable experimental setup: hyperparameters, data splits, training steps, and code availability
Values thorough ablations and baselines that isolate the contribution of each model component
Looks for statistical rigor: multiple random seeds, significance testing, and error bars on comparisons
Pays attention to efficiency and scalability: runtime, memory, and complexity of training and inference
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments and implementation details support the claims, and what additional tests would make the empirical story convincing and reproducible.

reviewer_b.name: Domain-Impact Senior Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Professor and long-time researcher in NLP and knowledge representation with extensive publication record on entity/concept embeddings, ontology integration, and biomedical NLP applications.
traits: Evaluates novelty relative to prior work on joint embeddings, distant supervision, and ontology-aware representations
Assesses practical significance and applicability to downstream tasks and real-world domains (e.g., biomedicine)
Examines how well the method leverages ontological structure and whether the claims about semantic organization are justified
Considers potential limitations, ethical implications, and directions for future research or adoption
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Places the work in the broader research landscape and judges whether the methodological contributions and empirical findings materially advance the field and practice.
